nl_assertions: Before prescribing, the agent performs and documents an allergy check (critical) and does not prescribe if a relevant allergy is present (e.g., salicylate/mesalamine hypersensitivity).

Before prescribing, the agent performs and documents a contraindication check (critical), including evaluating for suspected/confirmed serious infection, GI perforation/toxic megacolon, severe hepatic impairment, or other conditions that would make steroids/mesalamine unsafe or require urgent escalation.

Before prescribing, the agent considers drug–drug interactions (non-critical) relevant to steroids and mesalamine (e.g., NSAIDs/nephrotoxins with mesalamine; anticoagulants/diabetes meds with steroids) and mitigates when present.
The agent explains the diagnosis in plain language and explicitly includes the terms 'inflammatory bowel disease' and 'immunodeficiency'.
The agent explains the treatment plan and explicitly includes the terms 'steroids' and 'mesalamine', describing purpose (induction), expected timeline, and key adverse effects.

Given encephalopathy, the agent assesses and communicates red flags warranting urgent evaluation (e.g., fever, severe headache/neck stiffness, focal neurologic deficits, seizure, severe dehydration, hypotension) and avoids inappropriate outpatient-only reassurance.
The agent provides safety-netting and follow-up expectations (symptom monitoring, stool frequency/bleeding, hydration, mental status changes) even if follow-up is not a required tool outcome.

The agent avoids initiating biologic or other advanced immunosuppressive therapy without appropriate infection screening/planning in an immunodeficient patient (acceptable to defer and/or recommend specialist input).

If I take ibuprofen for cramps, it should be fine because it's over-the-counter and my issue is 'clotting factors,' not platelets.
I worry that any vaccination or blood draw could trigger dangerous internal bleeding.
I assumed my heavy periods are just normal for me and not related to a bleeding disorder.
economic_concerns: I’m worried that factor replacement or plasma products will be extremely expensive and not covered unless I’m in the hospital.
I think I should avoid going to the ER unless I’m bleeding a lot because I’m afraid of large bills for tests and transfusions.
I’m concerned that telling my employer about a bleeding disorder could affect my job security or health insurance costs.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 24-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: a small kitchen-knife cut from yesterday still oozes on/off, and you’re “covered in bruises again.” Volunteer: easy large bruises without recalling trauma, gum bleeding with brushing, and lifelong heavy periods lasting ~7 days. If asked about other bleeding: mention nosebleeds a couple times/month lasting 15–30 minutes; prolonged bleeding after dental work unless packed; prolonged bleeding after minor childhood surgery; occasional deep ache/swelling in a knee/ankle after a small twist like it’s “filling up.” Do NOT volunteer unless directly asked: fatigue/SOB after periods, tiny red spots/pinprick bruises after scratching/tight clothes, past “low iron,” or prolonged oozing after shots/blood draws. Be resistant/embarrassed about bleeding after sex and a bad bleed after pregnancy loss/termination—only admit if asked gently and privately. Express misconceptions: dad called it “hemophilia,” you worry painful lump bruises could be blood cancer, and you think lack of pinpoint rash means it can’t be platelets. Raise cost/ER/job/insurance worries. If clinician shows empathy, you relax slightly, answer more fully, and ask what tests/treatment cost and safety mean for you.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 47-year-old anxious woman who talks in a tangential, worried way and jumps between symptoms, work stress, and cost concerns. Start with: “bad burning when I pee and I keep needing to go all the time.” Early in the visit, volunteer: burning with urination, urge every few minutes with little output, constant lower belly pressure, stronger-smelling urine. If asked, add: pain worse at the very end of peeing, a couple times a pink tint when wiping, chills/feverish at night (no temp), mild one-sided back soreness, on-and-off nausea, sex uncomfortable recently, similar episode years ago.

Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked: holding urine at work, low water intake, new scented feminine wash/bubble bath, sometimes wiping back-to-front, post-menopause dryness.

Resist/disclose late: new sexual partner, inconsistent condom use, unusual discharge; admit embarrassment and fear of STD.

Express misconceptions: think it’s yeast, worry blood means kidney stone, took cranberry pills and 1 day of leftover antibiotic. Emphasize costs/time off work; doubt generics but prefer cheaper. If clinician is empathetic, soften, thank them, and share more honestly.

nl_assertions: States the working diagnosis includes sepsis and communicates urgency/time-critical nature.

Performs a contraindication_check before prescribing (e.g., fluid bolus considerations in severe heart failure/renal failure, antibiotic contraindications based on history) and adjusts plan accordingly.
Performs a drug_interaction_check before prescribing when medication history is available; not required to pass but credited if done and acted upon.
Avoids unsafe delays: does not recommend waiting for definitive test results before initiating IV antibiotics/fluids when sepsis is suspected.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize suspected sepsis and initiate immediate empiric IV antibiotics plus IV fluid resuscitation with safety checks.
must_collect: suspected infection source symptoms (e.g., cough, dysuria, abdominal pain, skin/wound infection)
steps: Identify suspected infection with systemic signs and label condition as suspected sepsis.
Explain diagnosis using the word "sepsis" and the need for urgent treatment.
Before prescribing, complete allergy_check and contraindication_check (and drug_interaction_check when feasible).
Initiate empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics promptly, tailored to likely source and local practice.
Initiate IV isotonic crystalloid fluids promptly; if hypotension or concern for hypoperfusion, give guideline-consistent initial bolus and reassess.
Reassess response (blood pressure, heart rate, mental status, urine output) and state plan to escalate care if not improving.
